name: Cootie Williams
abstract: Charles Melvin "Cootie" Williams (July 10, 1911 – September 15, 1985) was an American jazz, jump blues, and rhythm and blues trumpeter.

concerts.label: Benny Goodman and His Swing Orchestra
Midnight Performance: Pittsburgh Courier Poll Winners Jazz Concert

works.t: Things Ain't What They Used To Be
Second Sacred Concert: The Shepherd (Who Watches Over the Night Flock)

concerts.label: Newport Jazz Festival: The Ellington Saga, Part I
Newport Jazz Festival: The Ellington Saga, Part II

about_quotes.speaker: Lawrence Brown
about_quotes.evidence: At the time you joined Duke, the 12 members of the band were Johnny Hodges, Barney Bigard, Harry Carney, Arthur Whetsel, Cootie Williams, Freddie Jenkins, Joe Nanton, Fred Guy, Wellman Braud, Sonny Greer, and Duke, is that right? Right. Uh-huh.

about_quotes.speaker: Jimmy Maxwell
about_quotes.evidence: And even when I was in Benny Goodman's band, I had a very bad period there... You know, you're working in a band, you forget who's black and who's white... Cootie Williams, five or six black guys

about_quotes.speaker: Jon-Erik Kellso
about_quotes.evidence: His main influences were not from current trumpet players of his day, but from early pioneers including Bix Beiderbecke, Cootie Williams and Louis Armstrong.
about_quotes.doc_title: Jon-Erik Kellso interviewed by Monk Rowe, Chautauqua, New York, September 12, 1997

about_quotes.speaker: Patricia Willard
about_quotes.evidence: When I joined Duke Ellington in the Cotton Club, it was ... Cootie Williams ... and we had Otto Hardwick ... Johnny Hodges, and Carney

about_quotes.speaker: Gene Ramey
about_quotes.evidence: I went with Cootie Williams in the club that Birdland owned over there on the East Side. ... I was with Cootie Williams.

about_quotes.speaker: John Pope
about_quotes.evidence: Yes, Cootie played with me quite a bit. Back he started right out playing with me right here in Mobile.
